dialogos.texto_en: Hmm, I wonder if they have canoes here?
There's no way they'd have that kind of souvenir.\nConsider the size, Yotsuba.
If it's a single-seater,\nmaybe you could take it home with you, right?
...There's nowhere to put it. Stop.
Hey, what are you guys talking about?
Fu-kun, can you stop Yotsuba?\nApparently, she intends to buy a canoe as a souvenir.
That's impossible...\nWhy does she want a canoe anyway?
At the Oceanic Culture Museum I visited earlier\nwith Nino, they had a lot of canoes on display\nthat people used in the past!
Ah, that's why.
It was a really interesting exhibit,\nand I can kind of understand why she feels that way.
It seems that the shape and size of canoes vary\ndepending on the region. There was also a reeeaaally\nhuge one that was made by combining two canoes together.
I think it was a canoe used for navigation,\nthey made it bigger to store food and cargo.
Of course you'd know that, Uesugi-san!\nThat's correct!
People over a millennium ago used canoes\nto migrate to islands in the Pacific.
It's amazing that they managed to paddle thousands of kilometers,\nthe people of the past were perserverant!\nI can't lose to them!
No one's competing.
However, it was thanks to that perserverance\nthat they were able to find new lands\nin the middle of the Pacific.
I heard that similar cultures\nsprouted up on distant islands. For example,\ncultivating taro or weaving clothes from banana fibers.
So I hear, the world is connected by the sea...
There may not be a single place in this world\nthat is truly isolated.
Some ecological systems can get isolated.
Huh?
Have you heard of the Galapagos Islands?\nOrganisms in isolated locations evolve independently.
Oh, yeah. I've heard of it.\nIt's also used to describe\na certain phenomenon, right?
Humans can travel across the sea in canoes,\nbut it's not easy for animals\nto swim to faraway islands. Plants can't move either.
Sometimes seeds can be carried across the sea\nby the wind, but it's not common.
There might have been situations\nwhere those people had no choice but to leave,\nand travel across the sea.
Come to think of it, once you leave,\nyou might never be able to return.
That's a good phrase.
Huh? What is?
It's just as you said.\nMaybe the idea of isolation is just a made up concept by humans.
I mean, I didn't mean to be that deep or anything.\nIt was just a random thought.
Exactly.\nIt means that the world is united.\nYou have a creative imagination, Nino.
D-don't flatter me too much, c'mon.
There might have been situations\nwhere those people had no choice but to leave,\nand travel across the sea.
Come to think of it, once you leave,\nyou might never be able to return.
That depends on human will, doesn't it?
Someone had the idea of exploring for a new land.\nWithout that, nothing would have started.
You need to exert effort in order to achieve the results you want.\nIf not you will lose your will to succeed.
I'm sure at first,\nthere were many things that didn't go well.
Yeah, they probably examined the causes\nof failure each time, thought of new methods,\nand improved the canoe.
It may have taken years, or maybe even decades.
Even so, they continued to hold onto their willpower\nuntil the end, and finally arrived\nat their destination.
...That sounds like a story.\nBut it might really have happened.
There might have been situations\nwhere those people had no choice but to leave,\nand travel across the sea.
Come to think of it, once you leave,\nyou might never be able to return.
Even if the one who left was filled with hope,\nI wonder how the ones left behind felt?
Who knows.
Ah, but, actually,\nthey might have just been absent-minded.
Absent-minded?
Maybe they got lost\nwhile searching for delicious fish\nand ended up on a different island!
...That's possible.
Well, if they traveled because of food, then we can say\nthat their appetite was something fierce.
Fu-kun, are you picturing Itsuki in your head right now?
...Don't tell her that.
Okay, okay.
